notes.note: Aulus Caecina of Volaterrae, man of letters and Cicero's close friend; a Pompeian who had returned to Italy after Pharsalus and was seeking Caesar's full pardon, which Cicero pursued through several letters (Fam. 6.5-6.9). Distinct from his father, the defendant of the Pro Caecina.

A proconsul was a former consul (or sometimes praetor) commanding a province with consular authority after his year of office; Furfanius held Sicily, a strategically vital grain-producing province, in 46-45 BC.

notes.anchor: his father, a distinguished man and a man of courage
notes.note: The elder Aulus Caecina of Volaterrae, the defendant whom Cicero successfully represented in the Pro Caecina (c. 69 BC), a landmark speech on Roman property law and the interdict 'uti possidetis'.

A standard Ciceronian circumlocution for the defeat of the Pompeian cause and the civil war's aftermath; by framing it as a 'calamity of the republic' rather than Caesar's victory, Cicero appeals to Furfanius's sympathies without naming Caesar directly.
